page.title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: 136 alterations and 146 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

supporting_publications.title: Carpal tunnel syndrome may be triggered by vitamin D deficiency and its severity was correlated with vitamin D levels.
This study suggests a potential link between vitamin D status and the occurrence of carpal tunnel syndrome in women younger than 50 years.
Vitamin B6 is therapeutic in treating carpal tunnel syndrome.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Mild CTS: spontaneous resolution in 30-40% within 12 months without treatment; moderate-severe: <5% spontaneous resolution; pregnancy-related CTS resolves in 80% post-delivery
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Surgical decompression (carpal tunnel release, CTR): 90% symptom resolution at 12 months; 85% satisfaction at 5 years; superior to splinting at 6 months (SEVIORAL trial); corticosteroid injection: 75% improvement at 4 weeks, 50% at 6 months, 20% at 12 months
remission.gap_size: Small — surgery is highly effective; the question is appropriate timing and selecting patients for non-surgical management
remission.barrier_detail: Surgery is underused in some health systems where patients are kept on conservative management (splinting, cortisone) for years with progressive nerve damage; conversely, overused in mild cases where non-surgical management could suffice; ergonomic intervention is chronically under-addressed
remission.last_soc_change: 2016 — AAOS CTS guideline updated; surgical release remains gold standard since 1970s; endoscopic vs open technique evidence established 2000s; no paradigm change
remission.outdated_treatments: Vitamin B6 supplementation (consistent Cochrane review evidence of no benefit; still widely recommended and sold for CTS); diuretics for CTS (water-retaining theory largely abandoned; little evidence); open release in all patients regardless of severity (endoscopic offers equivalent outcomes with faster recovery in selected patients)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Ultrasound-guided hydrodissection with PRP/saline (emerging technique for releasing adhesions without surgery — Phase 2 data); therapeutic ultrasound (Cochrane review: limited evidence but some improvement in pain); nerve gliding exercises; ergonomic assessment and keyboard modification
remission.frontier_signal: US-guided percutaneous needle hydrodissection (minimally invasive alternative to surgery — expanding Phase 2/3 data); steroid + hyaluronic acid injection combination; gene therapy for CRISPR-mediated median nerve regeneration (very early stage)
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
